event: Susanna bathing, observed by the two elders
symbols: water and bathing — purity and innocence
Susanna's flowing garment — modesty and virtue under threat
lurking elders in background — the corruption of authority and abuse of power
garden setting — the private, enclosed space violated by predatory eyes
trees — concealment and the hidden nature of the elders' wickedness
description: A detailed engraving showing a young woman (Susanna) wading in water, her body partially draped in flowing fabric that billows around her. She appears startled, turning her body as if aware of being watched. In the background, partially hidden among dense trees and vegetation, two bearded older men (the elders) are visible observing her. The scene is rendered in fine crosshatching with dramatic contrast between the brightly lit figure of Susanna and the dark, shadowed garden. Text is visible at the bottom of the print.
theological_description: The story of Susanna and the Elders (Daniel 13 in the Septuagint/Vulgate) is a powerful narrative about the vindication of innocence against corrupt authority. Susanna, a virtuous wife, was spied upon by two lecherous judges while bathing in her garden. When she refused their advances, they falsely accused her of adultery — a capital offense. Condemned to death on the basis of their lying testimony, Susanna cried out to the Lord: 'O eternal God, you know what is hidden and are aware of all things before they come to be; you know that they have testified falsely against me.' God heard her prayer and raised up the young Daniel, who cross-examined the elders separately, exposed their contradictory testimony, and saved the innocent Susanna. The story powerfully illustrates themes that pervade canonical Scripture: God as the defender of the righteous (Psalm 34:17-19), the danger of corrupt judges (Isaiah 10:1-2), and the promise that 'the LORD works righteousness and justice for all the oppressed' (Psalm 103:6). Susanna's faith in the face of false accusation and unjust condemnation typologically prefigures Christ's own trial before corrupt authorities.

scripture_refs.reason: The righteous cry out and the Lord hears — Susanna's prayer to God against false accusation
The Lord works righteousness for the oppressed — Susanna vindicated by divine intervention through Daniel
Woe to those who make unjust laws — the corrupt elders perverting justice
God hates a false witness and those who stir up conflict — the elders' lying testimony
